query: bleeding risk in von Willebrand disease patients during cardiac surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Heyde Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- The Role of Aortic Valve Replacement
retrieved_docs.content: Recommendation by expert consensus advises that aortic valve replacement be considered the first-line treatment in the management of Heyde syndrome. [14] Aortic valve replacement has demonstrated high rates of cessation of recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding compared to other therapies. [8] [15] Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) is preferred over surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR), as TAVR is a safer modality that offers a lower postoperative risk and eliminates the chance of HMWM VWF reduction as a result of cardiovascular bypass that would be required in SAVR. [16] There is currently insufficient data regarding the use of post-procedural anticoagulation after aortic valve replacement in Heyde syndrome; rendering this decision should be on a case-by-case basis, which considers input from a cardiologist, gastroenterologist, and sometimes a cardiothoracic surgeon. Currently, most patients with Heyde syndrome who undergo aortic valve replacement are given a several-month course of dual antiplatelet therapy, in accordance with standard valve replacement guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: von Willebrand Factor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Some clinicians prefer vWF replacement therapy in patients with vWD undergoing major surgery and in high-risk populations, including pediatric patients aged 2 or younger, older individuals with extensive comorbidities, and people at high risk for thrombosis (eg, due to old age or cancer or orthopedic surgery). Secondary prophylaxis may be useful in patients with type 3 vWD with concomitant low FVIII levels who experience frequent hemarthrosis, frequent and chronic epistaxis, chronic arthropathy, and recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Stratification, Cardiac (Archived) -- Function -- Perioperative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anticoagulants must be determined considering the clinical history and the surgical procedure to be done. Surgical procedures with a low risk of bleeding may have anticoagulation continued. For patients with a high risk of thromboembolic disease (for example, patients with mechanical valves), it may be reasonable to continue anticoagulation if there is a low risk of bleeding. In general, therapeutic anticoagulants are discouraged because of their harmful effect on the ability to control and contain surgical blood loss [16] .

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Assist Devices -- Complications -- Hematological
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding is the most common complication, occurring in both the perioperative period as well as later due to the need for anticoagulation with warfarin [13] . Cardiopulmonary bypass perioperatively alters the coagulation cascades and impairs the normal clotting mechanism, leading to bleeding. Also, bleeding has been attributed to the association of acquired von Willebrand disease in LVAD patients, typically more than a week after the procedure [14] . This is usually reversible if the LVAD is removed [15] .

retrieved_docs.title: von Willebrand Factor -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Thromboembolism: In patients at high risk for thrombotic events, such as those with advanced age, cancer, or undergoing orthopedic surgery, multiple vWF doses should be administered with caution due to the increased risk of arterial thrombosis. Venous thromboembolic complications have been noted in some patients with vWD, which can be attributed to sustained high levels of FVIII from multiple closely spaced infusions of FVIII/vWF concentrates. Low-molecular-weight heparin can be used prophylactically in such patients. vWF should be used cautiously in patients at high risk of venous thrombosis.

retrieved_docs.title: von Willebrand Factor -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: FVIII can accumulate in plasma when multiple closely spaced infusions are administered for severe bleeding or prophylactically before major surgeries. This accumulation results from exogenous FVIII infused with the concentrate mixture and an increase in endogenous FVIII synthesized by the infused vWF. Persistent high levels of FVIII may heighten the risk of postoperative thrombotic events, such as deep vein thrombosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Von Willebrand Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The key to the prevention of bleeding is patient education. Patients should understand to avoid high-risk sporting activities or contact sports. More importantly, they should be told about the signs and symptoms of bleeding and when to seek medical help.

retrieved_docs.title: von Willebrand Factor -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of vWD aims to correct the dual defect of hemostasis—abnormal coagulation reflected by low levels of FVIII and abnormal platelet adhesion, indicated by prolonged bleeding time. Adequate restoration of hemostasis requires correcting deficient or dysfunctional vWF and low FVIII levels. Hematologists should be consulted for vWD and hemophilia management. Collaboration between surgeons and hematologists is necessary for optimal outcomes in patients undergoing surgery. Pharmacists are responsible for verifying dosing, and nurses should monitor for any transfusion reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Mechanical Aortic Valve Replacement -- Contraindications -- Absolute Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Severe bleeding disorders: Patients with conditions that predispose them to bleeding, such as hemophilia, severe thrombocytopenia, or other coagulopathies, may not be suitable candidates for MAVR due to the need for long-term anticoagulation. The risk of life-threatening bleeding complications outweighs the benefits of the procedure in these cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Stenosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: They are also at increased risk of bleeding, especially GI bleeding, due to acquired von Willebrand syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Management of Patients With Congenital Heart Disease -- Other Issues -- Surgery in Adults with Congenital Heart Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac intensive care units should postoperatively manage these patients with CHD and high-risk surgery. The significant risks during the postoperative period are bleeding, dysrhythmias, and thromboembolic events.

retrieved_docs.title: Heyde Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: It has taken several decades of study to elucidate the precise pathophysiologic basis of Heyde syndrome. The development of the syndrome involves insult at the level of the cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, and hematologic systems. In 1986, Gill et al. discovered that patients with congenital cardiac defects who had developed AVWS had abnormal blood clotting times, which suggested a causal relationship between cardiac dysfunction, changes to von Willebrand factor (VWF) and disorders of hemostasis. [7] Shortly afterward, a retrospective cohort study conducted by King et al. (1987) reported the cessation of recurrent bleeding in 93% of patients with recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding and aortic stenosis who had undergone aortic valve replacement, compared with a success rate of only 5% in those who had undergone surgical abdominal repair or resection. [8] T.E. Warkentin first introduced the hypothesis in 1992 that aortic valve stenosis may lead to AVWD and subsequent development of gastrointestinal angiodysplasias that predispose to recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding. [9] Currently, the most accepted mechanism of AVWS proposes that increased shear stress forces, particularly across a stenotic aortic valve, induce conformational changes in large VWF multimers that result in the exposure of the A2 domain. Exposure of this domain allows the enzyme ADAMSTS13 to cleave large VWF multimers into smaller fragments, which interferes with native inhibition of angiogenesis and the delivery of clotting factor VIII throughout the body. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Cardiac Management -- Issues of Concern -- Anticoagulants
retrieved_docs.content: Thromboembolic risk and the risk of bleeding relative to the type of surgery to be performed should be weighed in each patient treated with anticoagulants. In high thromboembolic risk cases with a low risk of surgical bleeding (e.g., skin surgery), anticoagulant therapy may not be discontinued. In surgeries with a high risk of bleeding, anticoagulant therapy should be discontinued. In these cases, if there is a patient with a high risk of thromboembolic complications, the anticoagulants should be dosed so that the effect remains only until a few hours before surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Coagulopathies -- Clinical Significance -- von Willebrand Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The disease typically presents with nose bleeds, menorrhagia, easy bruising, gastrointestinal bleeding, and excessive bleeding following procedures such as dental, tonsillectomy, and adenoidectomy, except the rare type III, which presents with joint bleeds such as hemophilia A or B.

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrinogen -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac surgery: Patients undergoing cardiovascular surgery involving cardiopulmonary bypass often have perioperative coagulopathic bleeding, which requires transfusion of blood and blood products. The multiple risk factors affecting bleeding include the type of procedure, bypass time, re-operation, and comorbidities. Pre-operative fibrinogen levels are an independent predictor of perioperative bleeding and transfusion requirement. Studies have reported the role of fibrinogen concentrate in reducing transfusion requirements in major aortic and coronary artery bypass graft surgeries. [10] [11] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1661
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is a hemostatic agent (isa), meaning it is classified among medications that promote clot formation. By increasing plasma levels of factor VIII and von Willebrand factor and enhancing platelet adhesion, desmopressin is used to treat or prevent bleeding in conditions such as mild hemophilia A, von Willebrand disease, and in patients undergoing surgery or on antiplatelet therapy. Its role as a hemostatic drug makes it an important therapeutic option for managing bleeding disorders and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as نقل الدم, is classified as نقل الدم and is recognized as a potential cause of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care. Awareness of this relationship is important for minimizing transfusion‑related risks and improving patient safety during operative and clinical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Kyselina tranexamicová, an antifibrinolytic medication, is contraindicated in patients with abnormalities of the faktory krevní koagulace (blood coagulation factors). Because tranexamic acid can promote clot stability, its use should be avoided in individuals who have deficiencies or disorders of these coagulation factors, as it may increase the risk of thrombotic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is a specialized therapeutic procedure for heart diseases (الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب). However, CABG is contraindicated in the presence of the disease ketorolak, indicating that patients diagnosed with ketorolak should not undergo this surgical intervention. This knowledge helps clinicians select appropriate candidates for CABG and avoid performing the procedure when ketorolak is present, thereby improving patient safety and treatment outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2838
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as نقل الدم, is classified as a type of Physical Treatment Methods, placing it within the broader category of physical therapeutic interventions. While blood transfusion can be a valuable clinical procedure, it is also recognized as a potential cause of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care. Awareness of its classification under physical treatment methods and its possible role in postoperative or procedural complications is essential for clinicians to manage risks and optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1454
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) and congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) are both classified as forms of heart valve disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). Recognizing that these conditions fall under the broader category of heart valve disease is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are plasma‑expanding agents that can help prevent venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). However, both Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are contraindicated in patients who have thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). This dual relationship underscores the importance of evaluating a patient’s underlying thrombotic condition before administering either Dextran 70 or Dextran 75, as their antithrombotic benefit in the venous system must be balanced against the risk of worsening an existing thrombotic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2058
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII is used as a therapeutic agent to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, helping to correct deficient clotting activity and control bleeding. By supplementing the missing or insufficient factor VIII, it restores normal hemostasis in conditions such as hemophilia A and other bleeding disorders, thereby reducing the risk of severe hemorrhage and improving patient outcomes.
